title: curve boundary function
content_html:
curve boundary function

parameter values control the boundaries of the ink line

quick - short narrow natural - contained wild - could go anywhere

so define circle segments for each of these return a curve options object

Boundary function just takes a single parameter ‘start point’

then we can draw the curve

create a new function demo_createLineBounded which draws a vector field line (like demo_CreateLine) using the setLineBoundary function Below is an example of a new function, **demo_createLineBounded()**, that uses the existing **setLineBoundary** function to create a bounded curve (a CurveOptions object) and then draws a vector field line using **createLine_VectorField**.

Explanation • LineOptions: Specifies the number of sample points and the base line width. • Ink/VectorInkOptions: Combines generic ink parameters (like inkFraction, decayType, exponent, etc.) with vector-specific parameters (distortionScale and noiseScale). These control how the stroke decays and how much distortion is applied. • setLineBoundary: This function (already defined elsewhere) creates a bounded curve (CurveOptions object) based on a given start point. It automatically calculates default control points and an end point relative to that start. • BezierCurve: The createBezierCurve function (also defined elsewhere) converts the CurveOptions into a BezierCurve object that is used by the drawing function. • createLine_VectorField: Draws a smooth stroke along the Bezier curve and then applies a noise-based distortion to simulate organic, wavy ink edges. • Labeling: A text label (“Vector Field Bounded”) is drawn to the left of the start point for clarity.

Call demo_createLineBounded() (for example, from your setup or key press handler) to see the bounded vector field line in your sketch.

—————

•	**Function Signature Change:**

The function now accepts a String style in addition to the start point. • Conditional Branching: • If style is "quick", it uses smaller offsets for a rapid, short curve. • If style is "natural", it uses moderate offsets for a balanced curve. • If style is "wild", it uses larger offsets for a more exaggerated, dynamic curve. • If an unrecognized style is provided, the function defaults to the "natural" style.

This modification lets you control the curvature style by passing the desired style value when calling setLineBoundary.

————

the code produces the same drawing each run. why?

The drawing is identical each run because the parameters determining the curve and its distortion are fixed. For example: • setLineBoundary: When you call it with a specific style like “natural,” it always generates the same control points and endpoint relative to the start point. • Static Noise: In your vector field function, you’re using a constant value (staticTime = 1.0) and not modifying the noise seed. The Processing noise() function is deterministic (producing the same output each run unless seeded differently). • No Random Seeding: If you haven’t called randomSeed() or noiseSeed() with a dynamic value (like based on the current time), the pseudo-random number generators (both for random() and noise()) use their default seeds, which results in the same random sequence on each run.

If you want a different drawing each run, you could: • Call randomSeed(millis()) or noiseSeed(millis()) in your setup() function. • Or incorporate some additional randomness in functions like setLineBoundary (e.g., by adding random offsets to the control points).

These changes would introduce variation so that the drawing isn’t identical every time.
